notes: Author and memoir subject; born 1947, grew up in Chatham, Cape Cod, MA.
Kastner worked as a full-time computer operator at MIT's computing center in 1966-1967.
Kastner attended Cornell's School of Hotel Administration 1965-1966 and 1967-1968; operated the IBM 360/65 mainframe there.
Supplier of the punch-card tabulating equipment used at HEC, the IBM 1401, IBM 7094, and IBM 360/65 at MIT, and the IBM 360/65 at Cornell.
Early DEC PDP/8 minicomputers were appearing in labs around Cambridge in 1966-1967, signaling the beginning of the end of the glass-house era.
Employed nearly half a million people every night circa 1960 to sort checks through the financial system, illustrating the extreme labor intensity of pre-automation commerce.

successor: Acquired by Wang Laboratories ~1979; Wang bankruptcy 1992; Getronics (1999); now defunct
notes: Described as the largest data center in Boston circa 1968 and a serious software development house; Kastner's next employer after leaving Cornell.
The Hotel School's model hotel front desk ran on an NCR paper-tape cash register in 1965-1966.
Referenced as Kastner's future employer where Multics-derived timesharing techniques from MIT resurfaced; foreshadowed in this chapter.
Cornell's Langmuir Labs was described as 'a former GE research building' used as the off-campus data center location for the IBM 360/65.
Adopted Robert Chase's hotel-management simulation programs (CHASE/CRASE) for management training.
Adopted Robert Chase's Cornell simulation programs for management training.

successor: La Mancha Group/Wyndham (motel brand); Restaurants franchised then ceased operations (last closed 2022)
notes: Adopted Robert Chase's Cornell simulation programs for management training.

MIT research project developing foundations of timesharing and multiuser computing; Kastner's roommate worked there in 1966-1967.
Cornell Hotel School accounting professor who created CHASE, CRASE, and CHESS simulation programs. Inducted into the Hotel School's Hall of Fame in 2024. Taught Kastner first-year accounting.
Operations manager for Cornell's computing center; assigned Kastner to Sysgen Day in fall 1967.
Cornell systems programmer who worked with Kastner on Sysgen Day, September 23, 1967.
Located in Chatham, Cape Cod, MA. Grew from a small attached restaurant to six dining rooms (575 seats), cocktail lounges (200), and an opera house (300). June-to-September seasonal operation.
Kastner enrolled in 1965; returned 1967-1968. Site of Hotel Ezra Cornell (HEC) tradition and the CHASE simulation project.
